An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell 2 miles west of Pickwick Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Vorticity lobe rotated northeast across East Tennessee in larger parent circulation around Northwest Missouri upper level low pressure system. Convection became severe producing some large hail and wind damage in an area where cold air aloft was coincident with a low level jet and zone of low level convergence ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported in Ooltewah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave that began in July continued into early August. Heat index values climbed to at least 105 degrees on most afternoons from the 1st through the 16th.  The hottest temperatures were reported in Lincoln County at Fayetteville, which reached 102 on the 4th and 101 on the 11th.  Upper 90s were reported on all other days  through the 16th except for the 6th with 93 degrees.   Morning lows only dipped into the mid 70s on many of those mornings as well.  Higher elevations on the Cumberland Plateau were 5 to 10 degrees cooler during this period of hot weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105 degrees during the afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Crockett County. As a result several roads were flooded in the Alamo area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into the Mid South during the afternoon and evening hours on June 28th, 2010. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of the front. A few of the storms contained very heavy rain which produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Crump. Several roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving through the region produced an initial burst of two to four inches at several locations. As warmer air moved into the region, freezing rain followed the snowfall, resulting in a quarter to half of an inch of icing at most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported freezing rain produced 0.25 inches of ice on the roads in Rockwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Southeastern Henry County. Highway 641 was underwater as a result. Several other roads were flooded as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was situated from Northwest Tennessee into Eastern Arkansas during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 14th, 2010. This front interacted with a unstable air mass resulting in showers and thunderstorms across parts of Northwest Tennessee. Some storms became severe producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball size hail fell near Trezevant.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across Eastern Shelby County. Several roads were flooded including Houston Levee Road at Plum Creek Road near Lakeland. The intersection of Dexter Road and Lenow Road was flooded in Cordova. The intersection of Kirby Whitten and Century Center Parkway in Memphis was flooded along with Sycamore View Road and Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over parts of West Tennessee during the afternoon and early evening hours on May 23rd, 2010 due to daytime heating. The storms died out shortly after sunset. A few storms became severe with large hail.
A young boy took shelter from an approaching thunderstorm in a hot car.  He died en route to the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2 year old Manchester boy sought shelter from an approaching thunderstorm in a hot car.  He buckled himself in his car seat but was unable to unbuckle it when the storm passed.  His father found him in the car and rushed to the hospital but the boy died en route.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was underneath northwest flow aloft during the period between July 11th, 2010 and July 13th, 2010. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Kenton and started a fire. The roof was burned off the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued during the month of November across much of West Tennessee. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions to cover most of West Tennessee while Northwest Tennessee remained entrenched in extreme (D3) drought conditions. The biggest impact from the drought were hydrological concerns as many lake and river levels remained at low levels. Reelfoot Lake remained at one of the lowest levels in decades. Although some agricultural concerns remained as well as many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed livestock with baled hay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system moved across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on Sunday, December 12th through, Monday, December 13th, enough moisture was available across the mid state to produce a widespread snowfall accumulation event across the entire area. Total snowfall accumulations ranging from one inch in the Tennessee River Valley to as much as one foot across northern portions of the Cumberland Plateau occurred. This accumulating snowfall resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across all of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of total snowfall accumulation occurred in Joelton. Law enforcement officials reported that other locations across the county received between two and two and a half inches of total snowfall accumulation. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day into the early morning hours on Monday, December 13th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The below normal rainfall deficit that resulted in abnormally dry conditions across several counties in central and northwestern portions of Middle Tennessee in November ended by mid December, as ground moisture levels reached normal levels for this time of year.

With another below normal rainfall deficit continuing across several counties across northwestern portions of Middle Tennessee during the month of December, these counties continued to remain under the D2 Severe Drought Classification Criteria as stated in the U.S. Drought Monitor throughout the entire month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued during the month of December across much of Northwest Tennessee. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the Southern Plains during the late evening hours of December 14th, 2010. The system moved east into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of December 15th, 2010 and continued throughout the morning. Precipitation associated with the system fell in the form of freezing rain as temperatures had dropped to 32 degrees or just slightly below. Ice accumulations were generally less than a tenth of an inch and was mainly confined to elevated surfaces. However many bridges and overpasses became icy causing numerous accidents. By afternoon, precipitation either tapered off or changed over to rain as temperatures rose to above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced minor accumulations on elevated surface and on bridges and overpasses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system moved across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on Sunday, December 12th through, Monday, December 13th, enough moisture was available across the mid state to produce a widespread snowfall accumulation event across the entire area. Total snowfall accumulations ranging from one inch in the Tennessee River Valley to as much as one foot across northern portions of the Cumberland Plateau occurred. This accumulating snowfall resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across all of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of total snowfall accumulation occurred in Kingston Springs. A newspaper also reported that county 911 officials stated that 58 automobile accidents occurred across the county due to snow and ice conditions on the county roads. Details concerning the actual damage type and cost to damage to the vehicles were unknown. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day and into the early morning hours on Monday, December 13th as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As scattered snow showers developed across the Cumberland Plateau during the morning hours on Tuesday, December 7th, between one half to one inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred across Fentress and Putnam Counties, resulting in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across these two counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One half of an inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the lower 30s during the early morning hours on Friday, December 10th, water on surfaces that had collected moisture over the previous day remained frozen across Dickson County. At a chemical plant in Dickson, ice that formed on an industrial structure broke off due to its weight, and struck a value that then opened, and released a toxic chemical called toluene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around four hundred gallons of a toluene, a volatile chemical that is a water-insoluble liquid with the typical smell of paint thinners and that is widely used as an industrial feedstock and as a solvent, took place at the Quad/Graphics plant in Dickson, when a large chuck of ice fell and struck the handle, opening a valve of the facility's solvent recovery system. A company spokesperson said that the chemical drained across a concrete pad and into a grate, which lead to a drainage ditch where the spill collected. The ditch and grate were not connected to the public sewer system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a broad area of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee, mainly through the morning hours of Tuesday, November 30th, several counties experienced heavy rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding across several counties east of Interstate 65, with most of the locations affected being across the Cumberland Plateau Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Because of heavy rains and strong winds, some trees across the county were uprooted and blown over power lines and roadways. Lake Hills Road located off of Ovoca Road in Tullahoma was partially blocked in one area due to trees and water across the roadway. Coffee County Communinications Center officials had to send officers to the Pocahontas area in north Coffee county near State Highway 130 and several other areas across the county due to high waters. Tulllahoma police also reported water across Decherd Street, with multiple reports of debri washing across this road and other roads across the county also.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the West End Market BP in Livingston, at least several shallow rooted trees were uprooted due to strong winds.

A long-lived mesoscale convective system that contained heavy rain and thunderstorms enveloped the region during the late evening and overnight hours.  24 hour rainfall amounts from this system reached 3 to over 5 inches. Runoff and flash flooding from the heavy rainfall began during the late evening on the 29th and lasted through the morning hours of the 30th. The runoff pushed a few small creeks and streams out of their banks, flooding some roads, and submerging a school bus in high water.  Fortunately, no students were on the bus at the time. Several county schools were delayed due to the high water and impassible roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding forced the closing of Roark's Cove Raod and Beans Creek Road.  A school bus also drove into flood water on Collins Road, but fortunately no students were on board.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a broad area of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee, mainly through the morning hours of Tuesday, November 30th, several counties experienced heavy rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding across several counties east of Interstate 65, with most of the locations affected being across the Cumberland Plateau Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Old Allardt Road next to the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers produced snowfall accumulation of an inch or less in Lincoln and Moore Counties.  The snow began on the morning of the 12th following the passage of a strong Arctic cold front, with periods of snow showers lasting off and on through the morning of the 13th.  Wind chills also dropped into the zero to 5 below zero range during the morning hours of the 13th due to gusty northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals from a dusting to between 0.5 and 1.0 inch occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving through the region produced an initial burst of two to four inches at several locations. As warmer air moved into the region, freezing rain followed the snowfall, resulting in a quarter to half of an inch of icing at most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported 0.50 inches of ice on the roads in Blountville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the middle teens during the morning hours on Monday, December 13th, a 73 year old women who had mistakenly locked herself out of her house, died due to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Maury County Sheriff's Department reported that a 73 year old women was found dead from freezing to death after locking herself out of her home in northwest Maury County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system moved across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on Sunday, December 12th through, Monday, December 13th, enough moisture was available across the mid state to produce a widespread snowfall accumulation event across the entire area. Total snowfall accumulations ranging from one inch in the Tennessee River Valley to as much as one foot across northern portions of the Cumberland Plateau occurred. This accumulating snowfall resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across all of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county. A newspaper also reported that snow and ice that covered roads across the county also caused a car in Centerville to slide off the road and strike several trees just off of State Route 50, resulting in the death of the male driver. Details concerning the actual damage type and cost of damage to the car where unknown. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day and into the early morning hours on Monday, December 13th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

County law enforcement officials reported an average of five inches of total snowfall accumulation occurred across other portions of the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Mid-South on November 25th, 2010 and interacted with a warm and unstable atmosphere. As a result, showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and one tornado across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down along Jim Bob Scruggs Road, outside of Humboldt, and traveled east across Dollar Road and then lifted. The tornado caused damage to a dozen homes. Many of the homes had widespread roof damage. Multiple sheds were destroyed and several trees were snapped as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a strong wind field moved across the Tennessee River Valley. Strong southeast winds ahead of the front in combination with stable air over the higher terrain along the North Carolina border produced mountain waves and subsequent wind damage in the western foothills of the Appalachian Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 87 mph was recorded at the Camp Creek wind tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South had a favorable set-up for winter weather during the time frame of January 28th-January 30th, 2010. A cold front moved through the area on January 27th allowing cold arctic air to filter into the region behind the front. Meanwhile, a powerful low pressure system developed over the Southwestern United States. The system pushed east into Texas and eventually in the Lower Mississippi Valley bringing abundant moisture to the area. Precipitation, mainly in the form of freezing rain, began over Northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel during the late evening hours of January 28th. The precipitation spread east during the morning of January 29th and became heavier producing a wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow over much of the Mid-South with the exception of extreme southern portions of the Mid-South. As the air cooled further, precipitation switched to all snow. However, temperatures began to warm during the afternoon and evening hours changing precipitation back to freezing rain, sleet or just rain. Precipitation tapered off to freezing drizzle or flurries by the early morning hours of January 30th. The winter weather produced treacherous roads which caused many accidents. In addition, accumulations of ice produced numerous downed trees and power lines resulting in many power outages. Snow accumulations ranged from an inch across Southwest Tennessee to eight inches across Northwest Tennessee. Ice accumulations ranged from a trace across Northwest Tennessee to up to an inch across the southern tier of counties in West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to four inches of snow fell across Fayette County on top of a half inch to an inch of ice.

The precipitation spread east during the morning of January 29th and became heavier producing a wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow over much of the Mid-South with the exception of extreme southern portions of the Mid-South. As the air cooled further, precipitation switched to all snow. However, temperatures began to warm during the afternoon and evening hours changing precipitation back to freezing rain, sleet or just rain. Precipitation tapered off to freezing drizzle or flurries by the early morning hours of January 30th. The winter weather produced treacherous roads which caused many accidents. In addition, accumulations of ice produced numerous downed trees and power lines resulting in many power outages. Snow accumulations ranged from an inch across Southwest Tennessee to eight inches across Northwest Tennessee. Ice accumulations ranged from a trace across Northwest Tennessee to up to an inch across the southern tier of counties in West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across Gibson County on top of a tenth to a quarter inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most profound cold snap to occur in at least 14 years over West Tennessee occurred during the first two weeks of January 2010. Highs in Memphis stayed below freezing for 11 out of 13 days between January 2nd and January 14th. Lows in Memphis dropped into the single digits for two out of 3 nights between January 8th-January 10th. At least five deaths occurred due to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold weather killed two people in Memphis and one in Bartlett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system moved across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on Sunday, December 12th through, Monday, December 13th, enough moisture was available across the mid state to produce a widespread snowfall accumulation event across the entire area. Total snowfall accumulations ranging from one inch in the Tennessee River Valley to as much as one foot across northern portions of the Cumberland Plateau occurred. This accumulating snowfall resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across all of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and three tenths of an inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day and into the early morning hours on Monday, December 13th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day and into the early morning hours on Monday, December 13th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours on Friday, December 24th through the early morning hours on Saturday, December 25th. A broad upper level trough, and associated closed upper level low, moved across the mid state during the time period from late Friday night, December 24th, through  Sunday, December 26th. With surface temperatures in the lower 30s to middle 20s during this time period also, and an abundance of cold air aloft, a significant snowfall event occurred across most of the mid state. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged from one to three inches across the area, resulting in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the entire mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in roads throughout the county becoming hazardous due to the snowfall accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and one half of an inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in roads throughout the county becoming hazardous due to the snowfall accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours on Friday, December 24th through the early morning hours on Saturday, December 25th. A broad upper level trough, and associated closed upper level low, moved across the mid state during the time period from late Friday night, December 24th, through  Sunday, December 26th. With surface temperatures in the lower 30s to middle 20s during this time period also, and an abundance of cold air aloft, a significant snowfall event occurred across most of the mid state. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged from one to three inches across the area, resulting in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the entire mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Narrow bands of snow showers and flurries redeveloped after midnight on the 26th as a strong upper upper level low dropped southeast across the Tennessee Valley.   Most of the snowfall remained light with visibilities between 2-5 miles.  New snowfall accumulations ranged from a dusting up to one inch.||Already slick road conditions due to re-freezing of wet roads from the previous day's snowfall became even more slick, especially during the morning hours.  Temperatures struggled to climb into the upper 20s, with higher elevations even colder.  Some sunshine broke through for a period during the afternoon between intermittent snow showers and flurries.  This melted the snow on roadways, but also produced wet roads which would re-freeze into icy patches again during the evening and nighttime of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around an inch or less was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a broad area of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee, mainly through the morning hours of Tuesday, November 30th, several counties experienced heavy rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding across several counties east of Interstate 65, with most of the locations affected being across the Cumberland Plateau Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rock slide was reported along Highway 85 at Wilder. Several back roads were also closed across Fentress County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A one lane concrete bridge on Busccuffle Road near Wartrace was totally covered with water, with a massive pile of driftwood creating a logjam on the upstream side. A black Honda, which was driven by a passenger, was trying to ease into the water covered area near this bridge so that the vehicle's driver could rescue a Labrador dog. The car was swept away and ended up stuck in a flooded nearby ditch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed in a warm advection zone during the early morning hours on the 14th.  The snow showers mixed at times with rainfall before changing to all rain by late morning.  Quick bursts of moderate to heavy snow were reported resulting in isolated accumulation of 1 to 2 inches in southern middle Tennessee, especially atop the Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to inch of snowfall accumulation was reported across the county.  The highest reported snowfall total of 1 inch occurred in Sewanee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South had a favorable set-up for winter weather during the time frame of January 28th-January 30th, 2010. A cold front moved through the area on January 27th allowing cold arctic air to filter into the region behind the front. Meanwhile, a powerful low pressure system developed over the Southwestern United States. The system pushed east into Texas and eventually in the Lower Mississippi Valley bringing abundant moisture to the area. Precipitation, mainly in the form of freezing rain, began over Northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel during the late evening hours of January 28th. The precipitation spread east during the morning of January 29th and became heavier producing a wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow over much of the Mid-South with the exception of extreme southern portions of the Mid-South. As the air cooled further, precipitation switched to all snow. However, temperatures began to warm during the afternoon and evening hours changing precipitation back to freezing rain, sleet or just rain. Precipitation tapered off to freezing drizzle or flurries by the early morning hours of January 30th. The winter weather produced treacherous roads which caused many accidents. In addition, accumulations of ice produced numerous downed trees and power lines resulting in many power outages. Snow accumulations ranged from an inch across Southwest Tennessee to eight inches across Northwest Tennessee. Ice accumulations ranged from a trace across Northwest Tennessee to up to an inch across the southern tier of counties in West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across Carroll County on top of a tenth of an inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered supercell thunderstorms produced large hail and a tornado during the late afternoon hours ahead of a cold front.  The tornadic storm affected southeastern Franklin County including Sewanee.  The storm environment exhibited strong wind shear and moderate instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Vertically stacked low pressure system moved across the Tennessee Valley during the early evening hours in a strongly sheared but weakly to moderately buoyant atmosphere across Southeast Tennessee. Wind, hail, and even a weak tornado occurred in this environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado with maximum speeds of 70 mph produced a damage path one half mile long and 50 yards in width in Monteagle just south of interstate 24. A few trees were snapped off and trained spotters reported significant debris flying into the air in the path of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east from Texas into Central Mississippi February 8th and 9th, 2010. Cold arctic air was already in place during the early morning hours of February 8th when a mesoscale convective snow band developed ahead of the approaching low pressure system over Central Arkansas. The band moved across East Central Arkansas into extreme North Mississippi and extreme Southwest Tennessee shortly before sunrise. This initial band of snow produced snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches. Additional snow bands moved into the Mid-South later on in the morning as the low pressure system tracked east. Heavy snow from these bands fell across portions of Northwest Tennessee. Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches with the heaviest falling between Dyersburg and Millington. Areas south and east of the heavy snow area saw precipitation change from snow to sleet and eventually to all rain as warmer air ahead of the low pressure system moved into the region. Some sleet accumulations occurred as well. Precipitation ended as flurries during the early morning hours of February 9th. The snow covered roads created hazardous driving conditions which caused numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across Haywood County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South had a favorable set-up for winter weather during the time frame of January 28th-January 30th, 2010. A cold front moved through the area on January 27th allowing cold arctic air to filter into the region behind the front. Meanwhile, a powerful low pressure system developed over the Southwestern United States. The system pushed east into Texas and eventually in the Lower Mississippi Valley bringing abundant moisture to the area. Precipitation, mainly in the form of freezing rain, began over Northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel during the late evening hours of January 28th. The precipitation spread east during the morning of January 29th and became heavier producing a wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow over much of the Mid-South with the exception of extreme southern portions of the Mid-South. As the air cooled further, precipitation switched to all snow. However, temperatures began to warm during the afternoon and evening hours changing precipitation back to freezing rain, sleet or just rain. Precipitation tapered off to freezing drizzle or flurries by the early morning hours of January 30th. The winter weather produced treacherous roads which caused many accidents. In addition, accumulations of ice produced numerous downed trees and power lines resulting in many power outages. Snow accumulations ranged from an inch across Southwest Tennessee to eight inches across Northwest Tennessee. Ice accumulations ranged from a trace across Northwest Tennessee to up to an inch across the southern tier of counties in West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow fell across Decatur County on top of a tenth to a half inch of ice. The heavy snow caused a roof of a boat dock near Sugar Tree to collapse. Five boats sunk as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in downtown Lafayette.

This initial band of snow produced snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches. Additional snow bands moved into the Mid-South later on in the morning as the low pressure system tracked east. Heavy snow from these bands fell across portions of Northwest Tennessee. Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches with the heaviest falling between Dyersburg and Millington. Areas south and east of the heavy snow area saw precipitation change from snow to sleet and eventually to all rain as warmer air ahead of the low pressure system moved into the region. Some sleet accumulations occurred as well. Precipitation ended as flurries during the early morning hours of February 9th. The snow covered roads created hazardous driving conditions which caused numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across Lauderdale County.

This resulted in multiple roads across these counties becoming snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents.

Multiple roads across the county became snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents. Details on any damage amounts or injuries associated with these accidents were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system moved across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on Sunday, December 12th through, Monday, December 13th, enough moisture was available across the mid state to produce a widespread snowfall accumulation event across the entire area. Total snowfall accumulations ranging from one inch in the Tennessee River Valley to as much as one foot across northern portions of the Cumberland Plateau occurred. This accumulating snowfall resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across all of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of total snowfall accumulation was reported in Christiana. Other COOP Observers reported between one and one and one half inch averages across other areas of the county. A newspaper also reported that Murfreesboro City Police reported 28 auto accidents during a span from 2 p.m. to 7 p.m., with seven of those accidents having injuries, while 21 of them were listed as non-injury. Details concerning the type of individual injuries to the seven individuals were unkown. For this and other portions of the county, this total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day and into the early morning hours on Monday, December 13th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As surface temperatures remained at or close to freezing during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th, and with an upper level disturbances passing across Middle Tennessee during this time period also, there was enough moisture across the mid state to ultimately produce scattered areas of ice accumulation. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th and throughout Thursday, December 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All exposed surfaces had ice accumulations of just under one tenth of an inch due to freezing rain across the county. This total ice accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county on Thursday, December 16th, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county during the late afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th and throughout Thursday, December 16th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the ice accumulations on them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As surface temperatures remained at or close to freezing during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th, and with an upper level disturbances passing across Middle Tennessee during this time period also, there was enough moisture across the mid state to ultimately produce scattered areas of ice accumulation. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th and throughout Thursday, December 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads across the county were covered with at least one quarter of an inch of ice, with locally higher amounts reported near the Cumberland/Putnam County line north of Interstate 40 due to freezing rain. This resulted in multiple roads being declared impassable, along with multiple wrecks being reported across the county. Details concerning damage type and amounts to vehicles were unknown. This total ice accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county on Thursday, December 16th, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th and throughout Thursday, December 16th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the ice accumulations on them.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple back roads across the county were reported to have a sheet of ice on them. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between three and five inches of total snowfall accumulation was reported across the county. Main and secondary roads were treacherous and numerous non-injury accidents across the county kept the Macon County Sheriff's Department busy throughout the day and night. Details concerning amount of damage to individual vehicles and damage cost associated with these accidents were unknown. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures across the county, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across the county for the remainder of that day and into the early morning hours on Monday, December 13th, as travel across roads throughout the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours on Friday, December 24th through the early morning hours on Saturday, December 25th. A broad upper level trough, and associated closed upper level low, moved across the mid state during the time period from late Friday night, December 24th, through  Sunday, December 26th. With surface temperatures in the lower 30s to middle 20s during this time period also, and an abundance of cold air aloft, a significant snowfall event occurred across most of the mid state. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged from one to three inches across the area, resulting in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the entire mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of total snowfall accumulation occurred across the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in roads throughout the county becoming hazardous due to the snowfall accumulation.

With surface temperatures in the lower 30s to middle 20s during this time period also, and an abundance of cold air aloft, a significant snowfall event occurred across most of the mid state. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged from one to three inches across the area, resulting in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the entire mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of total snowfall accumulation occurred across southern portions of the county. Other locations across the county had total snowfall accumulations generally between one to three inches. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in roads throughout the county becoming hazardous due to the snowfall accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As surface temperatures remained at or close to freezing during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th, and with an upper level disturbances passing across Middle Tennessee during this time period also, there was enough moisture across the mid state to ultimately produce scattered areas of ice accumulation. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th and throughout Thursday, December 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice coating of around one tenth of an inch on all exposed surfaces occurred over the previous couple of hours in Centerville. Freezing rain was reported to be continuing to fall at the time of this report. This total ice accumulation resulted in public and some private primary, secondary, and higher education school closures in Centerville and the surrounding areas on Thursday, December 16th, along with closures or postponement of other civic engagements across these same areas during the afternoon and evening hours on Wednesday, December 15th, and throughout Thursday, December 16th, as travel across roads throughout this area became hazardous due to the ice accumulations on them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of April 23rd, 2010. Behind the warm front, warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico made for a very unstable atmosphere. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Eastern Arkansas and North Mississippi during the evening hours of April 23rd. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. These storms pushed out of the area by the early morning hours of April 24th, 2010 as a strong low pressure developed across the Southern Plains and pushed east with an associated cold front. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the low pressure system and cold front during the day on April 24th. Numerous storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding and tornadoes. The storms moved out of the area by the mid-evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down numerous trees in Midtown Memphis. Several of these trees fell near the intersection of Hawthorne Street and Galloway Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed and intensified in the northern Gulf of Mexico and tracked east-northeast across northern Florida. Although surface air temperatures were in the 40s much of the early morning (nighttime) hours, moderate to heavy snowfall was able to rapidly cool air temperatures to at or just above freezing across southern Tennessee. In the higher elevations, temperatures managed to remain near or just below freezing during the entire day. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 inches.  Snow remained on the ground through the morning of the 4th on the Cumberland Plateau where temperatures remained near or just below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulation of 2.0 inches was reported in Lynchburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system moved northeastward from the gulf coast region during the early morning hours, into eastern Georgia during the afternoon hours, and just off the Carolina coast during the evening hours, on Tuesday, March 2nd, heavy snowfall accumulations occurred across locations in Grundy and Cumberland Counties of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall accumulation, estimated at three inches, occurred in Fairfield Glade. This resulted in multiple roads across Fairfield Glade and the surrounding area to become snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents. Details on any damage amounts or injuries associated with these accidents were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved along the gulf coast spread a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain across much of the southern portion of Middle Tennessee, mainly locations along and south of Interstate 40. Ice accumulations of one quarter to one half of an inch on exposed surfaces where common across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter to one half of an inch of ice accumulations on trees, power lines, and other exposed surfaces caused scattered to widespread power outages across the county. Newspaper reported that in the Big Swan Area of western Lewis County, that so many trees were down in that area that the Lewis County Road Supervisor had to hire a contractor to help in the clearing. Meriwether Lewis Cooperative reported that at least 200 homes lost power for a few hours across the county due to ice accumulations on power lines also.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter of an inch of ice accumulations on trees, power lines, and other exposed surfaces caused scattered to widespread power outages across the southern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system moved west to east across Middle Tennessee, a large area of showers with embedded thunderstorms developed which produced hail, flash flooding across the Cumberland Plateau Region, and a weak tornado in Robertson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the South Central Growers Greenhouse near the Springfield-Robertson County Airport and Highway 41. The tornado then continued|on a 3.6 mile path northeast. The last evidence of damage was at a residence just off of Barren Plains Road, also know as State Highway 25. The greenhouse roof was damaged near the beginning of the damage path. The most significant damage was near State Highway 25 where the roof of a barn was ripped off and a small shed was destroyed. A few trees were also snapped at the residence. Some farm equipment was also damaged. Very little damage was noted with the mobile home. This was the first recorded January tornado in Robertson County in more than ten years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet and snow began Friday night and continued through the day on Saturday. Between 1 and 5 inches of sleet and snow accumulation ocurred from this system in Lincoln and Moore counties, with the highest totals in the northern portion of these counties. Also, in Franklin county up to one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated on elevated surfaces and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of rain, sleet, and snowfall moved into the county Friday night before transitioning to a sleet, snow, and freezing rain mixture on Saturday.  Up to 3 inches of sleet and snowfall accumulation was reported in the Lynchburg area.

Between 1 and 5 inches of sleet and snowfall accumulation was reported, with the heavier amounts occurring in the northern portion of the county. Chestnut Ridge had the highest sleet and snowfall total of 4.5 inches.  Some minor icing was also reported in isolated locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of rain, sleet, and snow beginning Friday night transitioned into a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow on Saturday.  This produced sleet and snowfall accumulations  between 1 and 2 inches across much of the county.  In addition, in some locations up to one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated on trees and elevated surfaces.||Tragically, a former Madison county sheriff was killed in a car wreck while on duty during this wintry weather on Highway 64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passed to the south of Middle Tennessee, spreading precipitation across the area starting Friday morning, January 29th. Snow developed across Middle Tennessee and continued through 12 PM on Saturday, January 30th. Total heavy snowfall accumulations ranged from three to as much as nine inches across the mid state, which resulted in multiple roads across these counties becoming snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall accumulation, estimated at four inches, was reported across the county by the Cumberland County Sheriff's Office. Multiple roads across the county became snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents. Details on any damage amounts or injuries associated with these accidents were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed over southern middle Tennessee, north of a line of severe thunderstorms that was moving across north central and northeast Alabama. This severe thunderstorm produced hail up to half dollar size in Lincoln county Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail was reported in Petersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed and intensified in the northern Gulf of Mexico and tracked east-northeast across northern Florida. Although surface air temperatures were in the 40s much of the early morning (nighttime) hours, moderate to heavy snowfall was able to rapidly cool air temperatures to at or just above freezing across southern Tennessee. In the higher elevations, temperatures managed to remain near or just below freezing during the entire day. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 inches.  Snow remained on the ground through the morning of the 4th on the Cumberland Plateau where temperatures remained near or just below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of up to 1.7 inches were reported.  The highest amount of 1.7 inches was reported in Winchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of April 23rd, 2010. Behind the warm front, warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico made for a very unstable atmosphere. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Eastern Arkansas and North Mississippi during the evening hours of April 23rd. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. These storms pushed out of the area by the early morning hours of April 24th, 2010 as a strong low pressure developed across the Southern Plains and pushed east with an associated cold front. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the low pressure system and cold front during the day on April 24th. Numerous storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding and tornadoes. The storms moved out of the area by the mid-evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell in Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved along the gulf coast spread a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain across much of the southern portion of Middle Tennessee, mainly locations along and south of Interstate 40. Ice accumulations of one quarter to one half of an inch on exposed surfaces where common across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter to one half of an inch of ice accumulations on trees, power lines, and other exposed surfaces caused scattered to widespread power outages across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved through Middle Tennessee in association with a strong upper level low moving across the Southern Ohio Valley Region, a winter weather event developed across the area from the early morning through late afternoon hours. This event brought one to two inches of snow to a large part of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in multiple roads across these counties becoming snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall accumulation, estimated at one inch, was reported across the county by the Wilson County Sheriff's Office. Multiple roads across the county became snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents. Newspaper reported that the city of Lebanon's Police Chief received more than 40 calls concerning traffic incidents across the city, with incidents involving minor injuries or property damage. Newspaper also reported that the Wilson County Sheriff's Office received hundreds and hundreds of phone calls, so many that they could not keep count, concerning incidents associated with the snowfall. Also, an incident in Mt. Juliet occurred that involved 15 cars but no injuries resulted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level disturbance combined with a very unstable air mass to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Mid South on May 24th, 2010.  As convection developed throughout the day outflow boundaries were created that resulted in additional development until nearly all of the Mid South experienced at least some precipitation by sunset. After sunset the storms dissipated. Some of the storms were severe with large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level disturbance combined with a very unstable air mass to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Mid South on May 24th, 2010.  As convection developed throughout the day outflow boundaries were created that resulted in additional development until nearly all of the Mid South experienced at least some precipitation by sunset. After sunset the storms dissipated. Some of the storms were severe with large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of May 14th, 2010 and interacted with a very unstable air mass. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front. Some storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.

An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in excess of 10-15 inches produced flash flooding which later transitioned to a flood event across Gibson County. The North Fork Forked Deer quickly rose to record levels. Water spilled out of its banks and flooded areas near Trenton and Milan. Numerous homes and businesses were flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary setup across Western and Middle Tennessee late Friday night (April 30), and remained through the weekend.  A persistent southerly flow fed moisture into the area and precipitable water values rose to almost 2 inches, based on data from KOHX upper air soundings.  As a series of shortwaves moved through, a band of showers and thunderstorms developed and remained nearly stationary for much of the day on Saturday, May 1st and Sunday, May 2nd, resulting in widespread record flooding across much of Middle Tennessee.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe also, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage and seven confirmed tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buffalo River was reported to be out of its banks and still rising. Reported as the highest height seen in 20 years. Also, numerous homes, numerous businesses, numerous non comercial buildings, along with several county, state, and federal roads across the county received damage due to the flood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mr. Ritenour and Mrs. Linda Ritenour became surrounded by high water while Mr. Ritenour was driving a Chevrolet Suburban across a low bridge on their property, which is approximately two miles west of State Route 49 between Tennessee Ridge and Dover, along Upper Alsup Road. A neighbor saw the couple's predicament and alerted another neighbor, Steve Zywicki, who rushed to the scene to help. Mr. Ritenour tried to throw a rope to his neighbor, who could not quite reach it and was swept away. Mr. and Mrs. Ritenour, along with the Suburban, were swept away also. The Suburban was carried at least one half mile downstream from the low bridge. Mr. Ritenour survived after he managed to reach safety on one of the banks of the roaring creek. Both Linda Ritenour and Steve Zywicki drowned as a result of the roaring creek waters. Linda Ritenour's body was recovered approximately a half-mile downstream and Steve Zywicki body was recovered about 250 yards from the low bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On South Commerce Road, 8 miles east southeast of Lebanon, for a length of one mile and a width of 100 yards, damage associated with a microburst was evident. This damage included a barn being completely destroyed and another barn with significant roof damage. There were also two dozen mostly soft wood trees snapped or uprooted. Elsewhere in this vicinity, there was minor property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee, in association with a surface/upper air low pressure system moving across the area also,  weather developed during the mid to late afternoon hours across several counties in the mid state that resulted in small hail, heavy rain, and severe thunderstorms that produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davidson County Emergency Manager reported trees were downed in Bellevue and Belle Meade Communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee, in association with a surface/upper air low pressure system moving across the area also,  weather developed during the mid to late afternoon hours across several counties in the mid state that resulted in small hail, heavy rain, and severe thunderstorms that produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated winds at 55 knots in Inglewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient brought damaging non-thunderstorm winds to mainly the higher elevations in east Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported non-thunderstorm winds damaged a church steeple in Erwin.  Also many trees and powerlines along with a communications tower downed by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient brought damaging non-thunderstorm winds to mainly the higher elevations in east Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Greeneville Sun newspaper reported many trees and powerlines downed by non-thunderstorm winds.  800 customers lost power.  The Camp Creek Elementary School measured a gust to near 90 mph (78 knots).  In addition, a roof of a mobile home was blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system approached Middle Tennessee, and a surface low pressure system developed in northeast Louisiana, a warm, moist, and unstable airmass was ushered across the Cumberland Plateua Region of the mid state, resulting in a severe thunderstorm developing and producing large hail in Smith County. Other thunderstorms brought lightning damage to Putnam County and strong thunderstorms with heavy downpours to Smith County also, which resulted in a death associated with a vehicle accident due to hydroplaning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was reported in Carthage. Newspaper also reported that hail rained down across much of the county, and it was so intense, it piled up on roadways and on roofs of houses and buildings, forming a coating of ice in many areas of the county. The hail was so thick at times, that some motorist temporarily pulled off the roadways until the ice eventually turned into rain. Predominately, hail fell for generally 10 to 15 minutes before changing over to all rain. There were no widespread reports of damage to vehicles, homes, etc., resulting from the hail storm. Even though roadways were iced-over in some areas, there were no reports of wrecks involving serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance tracked across the region dumping heavy snow across the area.  Up to 17 of snow fell across the higher elevations, while 1 to 4 inches of snow was reported in the northern Tennessee Valley and Cumberland Plateau.  Less than an inch fell in the central Tennessee Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highway department reported around 5 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system approached Middle Tennessee, and a surface low pressure system developed in northeast Louisiana, a warm, moist, and unstable airmass was ushered across the Cumberland Plateua Region of the mid state, resulting in a severe thunderstorm developing and producing large hail in Smith County. Other thunderstorms brought lightning damage to Putnam County and strong thunderstorms with heavy downpours to Smith County also, which resulted in a death associated with a vehicle accident due to hydroplaning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As reported by the Tennessee Highway Patrol, Jessie C. Brewer, 69, of Rockwood, Tennessee, died after his 1994 Ford ASP hydroplaned and ended up running in front of a tractor trailer truck. Brewer was headed west on Interstate 40 at the 263 mile marker in Smith County, when he lost control of the car on rain slick Interstate 40. Brewer's car hydroplaned, ran off the right shoulder, and struck the guardrail, then slid back to the left across both lanes of traffic, struck the guardrail, then slid back to the left across both lanes of traffic again, struck the guardrail on that side again, and then ran back into the traffic lanes and into the path of a tractor trailer truck, which struck Brewer's vehicle in the left side. The driver of the tractor trailer truck was not injured. Any damage associated with the car and the tractor trailer truck was unknown. Actual depth of water on the road was also unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system moved northeastward from the gulf coast region during the early morning hours, into eastern Georgia during the afternoon hours, and just off the Carolina coast during the evening hours, on Tuesday, March 2nd, snowfall accumulations occurred across primarily the eastern half of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Van Buren County Highway Department reported total snowfall accumulation, estimated at 2.8 inches, occurred in Spencer. This resulted in multiple roads across Spencer and the surrounding area to become snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents. Details on any damage amounts or injuries associated with these accidents were unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall accumulation, estimated at one and one half of an inch, occurred in Crossville. This resulted in multiple roads across Crossville and the surrounding area to become snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system approached Middle Tennessee, and a surface low pressure system developed in northeast Louisiana, a warm, moist, and unstable airmass was ushered across the Cumberland Plateua Region of the mid state, resulting in a severe thunderstorm developing and producing large hail in Smith County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the Putnam County Sheriffs Department, the home of Tom Shoemake, on Overstreet Drive, was struck by lightning. The lightning strike caused damage to two vehicles, his yard, a tree, his driveway, the carport, and the electrical wiring in the house. A witness reported that the lightning first struck the tree in the yard, then appeared to travel through the root system of the tree into the concrete. When it hit the driveway, a big chunk of concrete hit a Chrysler car. The vehicle had extensive damage all over and three flat tires. From the driveway, the lightning traveled into the carport area, it blew out a concrete boulder. The boulder hit the ceiling of the carport, then came back down in the back window of a Chevrolet car. There was extensive damage to this car. The lightning then traveled into the electric wiring of the residence, blowing the door bell off the door. It also made a ditch in the yard and damaged the siding and gutter system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee, in association with a surface/upper air low pressure system moving across the area also,  weather developed during the mid to late afternoon hours across several counties in the mid state that resulted in small hail, heavy rain, and severe thunderstorms that produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Lawrenceburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of April 23rd, 2010. Behind the warm front, warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico made for a very unstable atmosphere. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Eastern Arkansas and North Mississippi during the evening hours of April 23rd. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. These storms pushed out of the area by the early morning hours of April 24th, 2010 as a strong low pressure developed across the Southern Plains and pushed east with an associated cold front. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the low pressure system and cold front during the day on April 24th. Numerous storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding and tornadoes. The storms moved out of the area by the mid-evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Memphis International Airport. Water depth reached the wheel wells of several cars at the intersection of Winchester Road and Plough Road. One person was injured when their vehicle hydroplaned into a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell across McNairy County producing widespread flash flooding. The city park in Selmer was flooded. Numerous roads and bridges were flooded or washed out. The flash flooding transitioned to flooding as the heavy rain caused rivers to rapidly rise and overflow their banks.

Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain produced widespread flash flooding in Gibson County. Rainfall amounts were estimated between 10 to 15 inches. As many as 16 roads were flooded or washed out. Highway 79 between Milan and the Carroll County line was covered by as much as 8 feet of water. In total, 137 homes sustained some kind of flood damage in Gibson County. Of those homes, six were completely destroyed. Eighty-seven businesses received damage with two businesses being completely destroyed. A few apartment complexes, mobile homes and other public buildings sustained damage as well. One fatality occurred as the waters quickly rose and swept one person off their feet. The flash flood event transitioned into a flood event as the heavy rain caused the North Fork Forked Deer and the Middle Fork Forked Deer River to overflow their banks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 10 to 20 inches produced record breaking flooding along the North Fork Forked Deer River in Dyer County. South Dyersburg was hit extremely hard with numerous homes and businesses sustaining major damage. At one point portions of South Dyersburg was underneath as much as 4 feet of water. In total, at least 600 structures received damage from the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain up to 20 inches produced widespread flooding in Henderson County. Numerous roads and bridges were flooded or washed out. At least 35 culverts were washed out from the heavy rain. As many as 43 homes sustained damage from the flood waters. Numerous rescues from homes and vehicles stranded in flood waters had to be made. The flash flood event transitioned to a flood event by late in the afternoon as heavy rain allowed rivers to overflow their banks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Old Reagan Road, southwest of Scotts Hill and tracked northeast. This storm cell had previously produced tornadoes in McNairy and Hardin Counties. A building and outbuilding sustained roof damage at the beginning of the track. The tornado continued northeast and hit Scotts Hill before crossing into Decatur County. Several trees were knocked down along the path. In total, five homes were damaged from the tornado.

Significant tree damage occurred along the track, especially on Brodie's Landing Road. Although minor damage occurred to most structures hit directly from the tornado, significant property damage occurred from falling trees. In total, 15 homes sustained damage. Three of these homes were completely destroyed. Seven homes received major damage while five had minor damage. One business had a roof blown off while one barn was completely destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front was located along the northern portions of the Mid South during the late afternoon and early evening hours of May 17, 2010 while an upper level disturbance moved across Illinois. These features combined with daytime heating to produce showers and thunderstorms across northern sections of the Mid South. Some of the storms were severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down numerous large trees in Dyer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across West Tennessee and Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of May 16, 2010 while an upper level disturbance moved into the Mid Mississippi Valley. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Dyer. Highway 77 and the Old Dyer-Trenton Road were flooded. The road in front of 184 West College Street collapsed and washed away.

In total, about 20 homes and three businesses received flood damage across the county. The hardest hit area was the North Woods Subdivision in Oakland. However, the cities of Rossville, Hickory Withe, and Somerville also had homes or businesses flooded. Several vehicles were stranded in flood waters as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain produced devastating flash flooding across Northwestern Shelby County. Up to 16 inches of rain fell causing widespread flooding. Millington was the hardest hit area where a levee holding back water from Big Creek was topped. As many as 200-300 homes in base housing at the Naval Support Activity Base were flooded. Numerous people had to be rescued. Four to five feet of water covered the navy base. Some locations around Millington were covered by as much as 8 feet of water. All roads in Millington were closed. Power outages occurred due to the flood waters.  Raleigh, Frayser and Bartlett also received widespread flooding where water was as deep as vehicles windshields. One fatality occurred in Raleigh as a vehicle became stranded in flood waters.  Water covered some streets in Downtown Memphis with water as high as vehicles wheel wells. In total, 576 homes received some kind of flood damage. Of those, 77 were completely destroyed. Eight businesses were damaged and 403 mobile homes were completely destroyed. Several other buildings including apartment complexes were damaged as well. The flash flooding event transitioned into a flood event where rivers rose rapidly spilling over their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced widespread flash flooding across Hardin County. One hundred roads were damaged as a result. Several water rescues occurred as a result of the flooding. Forty homes and twenty six businesses reported at least minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain, up to 12 inches, produced widespread flooding in Chester County. As many as 30 roads and bridges were flooded or washed out. The flash flood event transitioned to a flood event by late in the afternoon as heavy rain allowed rivers to overflow their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of Sulphur Springs Road and Steadman Road, southwest of Selmer, and tracked northeast. This tornado was spawned from the same cell that produced a tornado from near Ashland, Mississippi to near Pocahontas, Tennessee and just west of Ramer, Tennessee in McNairy County. Tree and minor roof damage was noted at the beginning of the track.  The tornado tracked further to the northeast and hit northern parts of Selmer. A carport failed due to fallen trees at the Christmas Tree Farm. A large home was moved off the foundation as well as a double wide mobile home. A single wide trailer was destroyed and wrapped around a tree. Total collapse of a few buildings occurred as well. Large trees were knocked down and minor roof damage was noted. The tornado then tracked further to the northeast and hit Bethesda and Purdy. Two churches were destroyed. A brick home sustained major roof damage while a horse barn was completely destroyed. The tornado tracked further to the northeast and hit the town of Good Hope. Two homes were destroyed. The tornado continued to the northeast crossing into Hardin County just to the southwest Milledgeville. At least ten injuries occurred in McNairy County from the tornado. In total, 22 homes were completely destroyed with 59 homes sustaining major damage. At least minor damage occurred to 219 homes. Fifteen mobile homes were destroyed and eleven sustained major damage. Twenty mobile homes received minor damage. Several other buildings and outbuildings were damaged or destroyed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front was located along the northern portions of the Mid South during the late afternoon and early evening hours of May 17, 2010 while an upper level disturbance moved across Illinois. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked trees on to homes and cars on Cades-Atwood Road between the towns of Cades and Atwood.

The flash flooding event transitioned to a flood event by late afternoon of May 1st. The South Fork Forked Deer River in Halls rose quickly above flood stage. Many homes and roads were affected by the high water.
