EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level disturbance combined with a very unstable air mass to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Mid South on May 24th, 2010.  As convection developed throughout the day outflow boundaries were created that resulted in additional development until nearly all of the Mid South experienced at least some precipitation by sunset. After sunset the storms dissipated. Some of the storms were severe with large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 59 mph gust just east of Union City. Several trees were knocked down by straight line winds in the Union City area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was measured at the Savannah AWOS. Trees and power lines were knocked down across Hardin County including Savannah, Pickwick, Walkertown, Childers Hill and Olivehill. Minor shingle damage was also reported throughout the county. A tree fell on a house on Country Club Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell during the day across Madison County. As much as 11 to 16 inches fell across the county. Forty roads and bridges were closed due to the flooding. Vehicles had water up to the steering wheels in some locations. Power outages occurred as a result of the flooding. Houses were inundated with water(as much as 5 feet) especially in South Jackson, the Herron Grove area of Jackson, McGee Loop area of Northeast Jackson, and Bemis. In total, 120 to 140 homes across Madison County sustained damage from the flooding. As many as 85 businesses in Jackson received damage. The flash flooding event transitioned into a flood event as rivers rose quickly from the rainfall and overflowed their banks. Some roads remained closed for days making travel difficult. Shelters were opened in Jackson due to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain up to 20 inches fell across Carroll County producing widespread flash flooding. Small streams and creeks overflowed their banks. At least 25 roads were flooded or washed out as a result. In total, eleven homes sustained damage from the flooding. Three of those were totally destroyed. Thirty-six businesses also sustained damage with nine being totally destroyed. Several other buildings including apartment complexes, farm buildings, and mobile homes also received damage. One fatality occurred when a vehicle was washed off the roadway as the driver tried to drive through flood waters. Water swept more than 20 used cars off of a car lot in downtown Huntingdon. Railroad tracks across the county sustained damage as well. The flash flooding event transitioned into a flood event as the heavy rain allowed rivers to rise rapidly and overflow their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front was located along the northern portions of the Mid South during the late afternoon and early evening hours of May 17, 2010 while an upper level disturbance moved across Illinois. These features combined with daytime heating to produce showers and thunderstorms across northern sections of the Mid South. Some of the storms were severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down numerous large trees and power lines across Trenton. The Lake Hayes area was also hit hard with several homes damaged and a car crushed by a tree. Extensive tree and power line damage was also reported in the Concord, Cades and Union Central areas. Several homes were hit by trees along Concord-Cades Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the south side of Humboldt and several houses received roof, siding and garage door damage. One home in Humboldt received partial roof loss and garage damage while the storage shed and wooden swing set were completely destroyed. Several power poles were knocked down before a winery received tree and vine damage. Some tiles were stripped off the business home at the winery. One rural home suffered more extensive damage near the end of the track with partial roof loss, porch removal and brick veneer ripped off the northeast corner of the home. Windows were also blown out on the home with a large well built brick supported carport completely destroyed. A well built 40 foot by 40 foot wood and metal shed on the property was completely destroyed with the wood support beams snapped off one foot above the concrete slab. A tour bus nearby was lifted and turned 180 degrees. A semi trailer on the property was also flipped over. Numerous large trees along the path were either uprooted or damaged. A few smaller trees were snapped off one to two feet above the ground. Several power lines were also knocked down before the tornado lifted near Pleasant Hill Road.

This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain produced widespread flash flooding across Tipton County. Up to 20 inches of rain fell causing devastating damage to homes, businesses, roads and bridges. In fact, 40 roads were impassable in Tipton County due to the flooding. Twenty-one of those roads and 14 bridges were washed out as a result of the flooding. Other roads received damage just from the heavy rain. Damage was particularly bad in Atoka and Mason. Numerous vehicles were completely submerged in water. Two apartment complexes in Covington sustained major damage. In total, 309 homes received damage from the flooding. Of those, 96 homes were completely destroyed. As many as 93 businesses were flooded and of those 21 were completely destroyed. Nineteen mobile homes were flooded with 14 being destroyed. Many other structures or buildings were flooded as well. One fatality occurred in Mason. The flash flooding event transitioned into a flood event as rivers rose rapidly and overflowed their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front was located along the northern portions of the Mid South during the late afternoon and early evening hours of May 17, 2010 while an upper level disturbance moved across Illinois. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees and power lines across Milan. Several homes suffered shingle damage and a railroad crossing gate was snapped off.  Four trees fell on a house on Mayo Cook Road north of Milan. Hundreds of giant trees were knocked down along Gann Road, Salem Road and Park Avenue. A couple of cars were also crushed by toppled trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees on Old Dyersburg Road south of Dyer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second warm spell of the season occurred across the Mid-South during the middle part of June. High temperatures reached into the mid 90s. The combination of temperatures in the mid 90s and high humidity produced heat indices around 105 degrees. One heat related death occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings were around 105 degrees during the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of June 16th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front across West Tennessee. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. By the afternoon of June 17th, the front began lifting back north as a warm front. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed. Some of these storms became severe as well producing damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down numerous trees in the town of Henry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours in association with maximum daytime heating on June 18th, 2010. The showers and thunderstorms would quickly dissipate after sunset with the loss of daytime heating. Some storms became severe with damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees at Houston Levee Road and Highway 64.

A strong thunderstorm produced knocked down a tree on Gum Creek Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees in Ridgely.

In Lincoln and Moore counties in Tennessee, strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the late afternoon and evening hours on May 1st. These storms produced knocked down a tree in the Waterloo area and produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall which produced flash flooding. Hardest hit were the extreme northern portions of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of highway 129 between New Herman road and Boonville road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low Pressure moved west of Middle Tennessee during the late afternoon and evening of April 24th.  A line of severe thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee ahead of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado entered Humphreys County just north of the intersection of Blue Creek and Crooked Creek Roads.  Damage consisting of snapped and uprooted trees continued almost nine miles northeast crossing Interstate 40 just east of the Highway 13 exit.  The last evidence of damage was along Barren Hollow Road just north of I-40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating resulted in isolated afternoon and early evening showers and thunderstorms across Arkansas on May 27th, 2010. A few storms were severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down numerous trees, power poles and power lines in Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved southwest to northeast across Middle Tennessee on May 14th, producing flash flooding, hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and a lightning strike to a structure that caused damage in Macon County.

A frontal boundary setup across Western and Middle Tennessee late Friday night (April 30), and remained through the weekend.  A persistent southerly flow fed moisture into the area and precipitable water values rose to almost 2 inches, based on data from KOHX upper air soundings.  As a series of shortwaves moved through, a band of showers and thunderstorms developed and remained nearly stationary for much of the day on Saturday, May 1st and Sunday, May 2nd, resulting in widespread record flooding across much of Middle Tennessee.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe also, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage and seven confirmed tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines downed on Railroad Bed Pike in Northern Lawrence County. Three poles were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked northeast into Hardin County from McNairy County and hit the town of Milledgeville. One house sustained major damage while another sustained roof damage. Minor damage occurred to a shed. Several trees were knocked down in the area. Two injuries occurred from the tornado. This storm went on to produce a tornado in Henderson and Decatur Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In Lincoln and Moore counties in Tennessee, strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the late afternoon and evening hours on May 1st. These storms produced knocked down a tree in the Waterloo area and produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall which produced flash flooding. Hardest hit were the extreme northern portions of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Harry Hill road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of June 16th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front across West Tennessee. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. By the afternoon of June 17th, the front began lifting back north as a warm front. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed. Some of these storms became severe as well producing damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees in Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed through the Mid South during the day on June 25th, 2010. A weak tornado also occurred in Hardeman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down northwest of the center of Bolivar along US Highway 64 and tracked through the intersection of Market Street and US Highway 64 near the Wal Mart and then quickly lifted. Four doors were damaged at an automobile garage. A used car lot had a column blown from the front of the office building. The roof of the Hardeman County Arts Council building was damaged and several trees were knocked down along US Highway 64.

A weak tornado also occurred in Hardeman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Brownsville. Several roads and a Wal Mart parking lot were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across the area triggered slow moving thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Storm reports contained both damaging thunderstorm winds and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High department personnel reported slow moving thunderstorms brought heavy rain to Elk Valley resulting in a minor mudslide on Lick Fork Lane. Part of the bank containing a tree slid onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across the area triggered slow moving thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Storm reports contained both damaging thunderstorm winds and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department personnel reported numerous roads flooded in Oneida from slow moving thunderstorms.  Some roads were under 2 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across the area triggered slow moving thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Storm reports contained both damaging thunderstorm winds and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management personnel reported numerous trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm wind near the intersection on Big Spring Gap Mountain Road and Beaver Hill Road north of Pikeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of June 16th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front across West Tennessee. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. By the afternoon of June 17th, the front began lifting back north as a warm front. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed. Some of these storms became severe as well producing damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew tree limbs down along the Poplar Avenue corridor from near Hacks Cross Road in Germantown to Byhalia Road in Collierville. In addition, minor roof damage occurred to at least one house in the area. Shutters were also blown off the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of June 16th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front across West Tennessee. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. By the afternoon of June 17th, the front began lifting back north as a warm front. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed. Some of these storms became severe as well producing damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Collierville. The intersection of Houston Levee Road and Frank Road was flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed into Hardeman County from Tippah County, Mississippi. The tornado tracked northeast hitting Pocahontas before moving into McNairy County. One fatality occurred in Pocahontas when a single wide trailer was destroyed. Two injuries also occurred. In total, one home was completely destroyed. Thirteen homes sustained major damage with another five receiving very minor damage. Two mobile homes were destroyed with another two receiving minor damage. Numerous trees were snapped and debarked in the area. In addition, power lines were knocked down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass across the area help trigger isolated severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Standard Banner newspaper in Jefferson City reported 2 trees downed onto 2 utility poles and powerlines by thunderstorms winds in Jefferson City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed into McNairy County from Hardeman County. The tornado briefly traveled northeast before lifting near Highway 57. Several trees and power lines were knocked down in the area. This storm would later spawn a couple of tornadoes further northeast in McNairy County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An old frontal boundary located across the area triggered isolated severe thunderstorms across east Tennessee during the afternoon hours.  Slow moving storms also produced flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported 1 large tree downed by thunderstorm winds in Sunbright.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure further intensified over the Mid-South continuing into the middle of August. The upper ridge of high pressure produced above normal temperatures over the area. The combination of high humidity and above normal temperatures produced brutal heat indices of 105-120 degrees. Four heat related deaths occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged between 110 and 120 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon through early evening hours on Monday, July 26th. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage across four counties in the mid state and and EF1 tornado in Davidson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service and Metro Nashville Office of Emergency Management Officials surveyed damage in Northern Davidson County. Most of the surveyed damage, including Trail Hollow Lane just west of Interstate 24, and communities along Brick Church Pike from Northbrook Drive south to Briley Parkway, was from microburst winds. A semi-trailer was blown over, dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted, and several roofs suffered minor damage. Maximum winds in these areas were around|80 mph.||Further north, areas along Westchester Drive experienced much more significant damage. Approximately 12 brick homes suffered heavy roof damage, including one which had a large section of the roof removed. Two buildings were impaled by two by fours, and a couple of dozen windows were blown out. The damage pattern in this area was characterized by convergence and obvious rotation consistent with a tornado.  Damage that occurred was also consistent with the highest end of the    EF-1 range, 105 to 110  mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a tropical depression interacted with a stationary front in Middle Tennessee, resulting in excessive rainfall across several counties east of Interstate 65 corridor. Area averaged between six and seven inches of rain, with some areas receiving as much as a foot of rainfall. Most of the severe damage across this area included impacted bridges, roads, and multiple types of structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to flooding in Gallatin.||Newspapers reported that seven people were rescued during the flash flooding in Gallatin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms formed in a moisture rich airmass across Middle Tennessee ahead of an approaching frontal system during the evening hours on Monday, July 12th. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, producing thunderstorm wind, flash flood, heavy rain, and lightning damage across several counties in the mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Warren County ambulance medics were injured when their vehicle hydroplanned on Interstate 440 near Nolensville Road in Nashville. The ambulance, which was traveling east back toward Warren County, slid into the median, crashed over the guardrail between lanes, and vaulted into the oncoming westbound lanes. It came to rest on its side. Investigators believed that heavy rain contributed to the accident, noting the ambulance was traveling at normal highway speeds when the crash occurred. The abulance was deemed a total loss. There were two people were in the ambulance. The ambulance driver recieved bone fractures but was in stable condition at the time of this report. Type and amount of bone fractures were not provided. The other paramedic was treated and released from Vanderbilt Hospital that evening. Details concerning type and extent of injury for this paramedic were not provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Mid-South on August 15th, 2010. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. Some storms became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees and tree limbs as well as a pizza restaurant sign in Bruceton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Depression 5 moved northward into the Mid-South during the day on August 18th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the Tropical Depression 5. Some storms produced very heavy rain which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Decaturville. Concord Road was closed as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved southeast into the Mid-South and interacted with remnants of Tropical Depression Bonnie as the remnants moved north. Numerous showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and evening hours of July 25th-July 26th, 2010 as a result. Some storms became severe and produced damaging winds, flash flooding. Behind the front during the evening of July 26th, a wake low developed that produced high winds over portions of Northwest Tennessee resulting in additional wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 3.5 inches of rain fell in a short time near Trenton resulting in flash flooding. Old Trenton Eaton Road was closed as a result of the flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding around Newbern. Numerous roads were flooded as drainage ditches were overfilled with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed across south central Kentucky and moved into the north central portions of Middle Tennessee during the early morning  hours on Monday, July 19th.  This resulted in some thunderstorms reaching severe thresholds, resulting in thunderstorm wind, flash flooding, and lightning damage across locations near and on the Northern Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For 2.7 miles along York Highway northeast of Gainesboro, numerous trees were snapped and some uprooted by straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed across south central Kentucky and moved into the north central portions of Middle Tennessee during the early morning  hours on Monday, July 19th.  This resulted in some thunderstorms reaching severe thresholds, resulting in thunderstorm wind, flash flooding, and lightning damage across locations near and on the Northern Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: House fire was started by a lightning strike which resulted in minor damage to a home in Allardt.  Extent of minor damage was unkown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracking through the mid South and Tennessee Valley brought a period of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern middle Tennessee during the pre-dawn hours of the 15th.  Snowfall accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were reported, with the heaviest amounts on the Cumberland Plateau in and around the community of Sewanee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations up to 1 inch was reported across the county.  The highest recorded snowfall total of 1 inch was reported in Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall fell during the month of September across much of West Tennessee. Memphis International Airport only received 0.14 inches of rain the entire month of September. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions into extreme portions of Northwest Tennessee. The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Hydrological concerns started to become an issue by the end of the month as many lake and river levels dropped. Burning permits were required due to the drought. A few wildfires occurred as a result of the dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions expanded into Dyer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Saturday, September 11th, a line of showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of this cold front. Some of the thunderstorms within this line reached severe thresholds, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage in four counties across the mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in the northeastern corner of the county, including State Highway 154 northeast of Jamestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure remained over the Mid-South during the middle to latter part of August. The upper ridge of high pressure produced above normal temperatures over the area. The combination of high humidity and above normal temperatures produced high heat indices of 105-115 degrees. One heat related death occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings reached between 110 and 115 degrees during the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, moist, and unstable airmass developed over much of Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Saturday, August 14th, and lead to the development of an atmospheric environment that was conducive for thunderstorm development. Some of those thunderstorms that developed reached severe thresholds during the late afternoon through early evening hours across four counties in Middle Tennessee, resulting in strong downdrafts that produced damaging straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heat index readings ranged from 110 to 115 degrees over much of Middle Tennessee on August 4th. There were no known fatalities. There were also numerous reports of damage from the heat, including exploding tires on automobiles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon temperatures around 100 degrees and unusually high humidity led to heat index values between 110 and 115 degrees on August 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracking through the mid South and Tennessee Valley brought a period of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern middle Tennessee during the pre-dawn hours of the 15th.  Snowfall accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were reported, with the heaviest amounts on the Cumberland Plateau in and around the community of Sewanee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations up to 2 inches were reported across the county. The highest reported snowfall total occurred in Lynchburg.

In addition to this, 70 homes were damaged. Extent of damage to individual homes was unknown. Some vehicles were also damaged, including a car that was nearly submerged when water covered the roadway on State Highway 293 near Ramsey's Market. A few swift water rescues were also preformed across the county. Many bridges and electrical services were also damaged. Extent of damage to bridges and electrical services was unknown. County schools were also closed and let out early on Wednesday, August 18th, because of the flooding.||Newspapers also reproted that tree structures were destroyed, 10 with major damages, 12 with moderate damage, and 30 with minor damage. Several small wood framed structures were also washed away. Several pets were also rescued from multiple flooded homes. Spring Creek overgrew its banks and caused additional flooding problems. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A young child died in LaVergne after being left in a hot car for more than three hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lisa Voransane, a 18 month year old girl, was left by her mother in a sliver Toyota Avalon for about three hours with the windows rolled up and the doors locked in          La Vergne. Outside afternoon temperatures between 12 PM CST and 3 PM CST were in  the mid 90s. Local police investigators re-created the conditions the little girl suffered while locked in the car and the highest temperature that they recorded was 117 degrees inside of the car. When the girl was first examined after being pulled out of the car, her body temperature was 106.9 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure moved through the Mid-South during the evening hours on October 24th, 2010. Elevated thunderstorms developed in association with the upper trough. The thunderstorms continued into the early morning hours of October 25th, 2010. Some storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced widespread damage for a 3 mile swath that was about 200 yards wide in Savannah. Numerous trees were knocked down, some were knocked down onto homes. Four homes sustained moderate damage with 8 homes sustaining minor damage. Several power lines were blown down as well causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a tropical depression interacted with a stationary front in Middle Tennessee, resulting in excessive rainfall across several counties east of Interstate 65 corridor. Area averaged between six and seven inches of rain, with some areas receiving as much as a foot of rainfall. Most of the severe damage across this area included impacted bridges, roads, and multiple types of structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous county roads were closed county wide. A total of seven roads are impassable along with two roads being washed out. Public also reported extensive flooding on Flat Gap Creek Road with one house having a foot of water in the house.||Newspapers reported that flooding damaged six buildings on Houston Road, and two on Flat Gap Road. Three of these were homes which were virtually destroyed. From three to four inches of water invaded the homes, covering floors with water, debris, and mud. The rushing water was even forceful enough to completely lift two buildings of their foundations, depositing one 150 yards away, right in the middle of Indian Creek Road. The other was stopped when it crashed into some trees.||Newspapers also reported that a small white wood framed building that was as a family/community house of worship on Flat Gap Road was shoved off of its foundation and pushed some distance. The house collapsed in on itself as it came to rest against some trees in the Vickery yard. Other little outbuildings were also washed away.     ||All of this damage was caused by total rainfall amounts of eight inches to as much|as one foot of water in the Flat Gap Creek/Houston Communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a tropical depression interacted with a stationary front in Middle Tennessee, resulting in excessive rainfall across several counties east of Interstate 65 corridor. Area averaged between six and seven inches of rain, with some areas receiving as much as a foot of rainfall. Most of the severe damage across this area included impacted bridges, roads, and multiple types of structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy flooding of low lying areas across the county destroyed tobacco crops that were planted in these areas. Thirty roads across the county were reported to have at least moderate damage, mainly across the eastern portion of the county, with 24 of those roads being closed. Per the wet soil conditions, several trees also fell across county roadways. Emergency officials evacuated about 50 nursing home residents from a facility close to a rising creek. Name and exact location of the creek was unknown.||Newspapers also reported that the counties highway department stated that 96 roads were damaged in the flooding, with 22 of those suffering moderate to severe damage.||All of this damage was the result of 7, to as locally high as 10 inches in a few locations, of rainfall across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, October 26th, an intense surface low pressure center known as a midlatitude cyclone developed across the upper Midwest, resulting in gusty surface winds, which produced strong to high wind damage events at some locations across the Middle Tennessee. As the day progressed also, a strong cold frontal passage occurred. Associated with this frontal passage was an outbreak of severe weather, resulting in several counties experiencing thunderstorm wind damage along with funnel cloud reports, along with near golf ball size hail being reported in Coffee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees were downed near the intersection of Valley View Road and East Jefferson Pike.

Juliet. ||Newspapers reported that the railroad track that carries a commuter train, the Music City Star, between Nashville an Lebanon had been washed out between Mt. Juliet and Lebanon. Several business, along with public buildings, in Mt. Juliet experienced flood damage, including David Deaton Karate Studio in the Valley Center on North Mt. Juliet Road and a Fire and Rescue station which is primarily used to houses ambulances behind Mt. Juliet City Hall. Extent of damage to these business were unkown. Floodwaters also covered little league baseball and football fields and also knocked over the new fencing around Charlie Daniels Football Field in Mt. Juliet. Heavy rains closed the Wilson County Fair, the largest fair in Tennessee, for the first time in its 157 year history, for Wednesday night, August 18th.||All of this damage was the result of four inches, to as much as one foot in some locations, of rainfall across areas mainly along and north of Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued during the month of October across much of West Tennessee. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions from extreme portions of Northwest Tennessee to much of West Tennessee while conditions intensified across Northwest Tennessee to extreme (D3). The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed livestock with baled hay. Hydrological concerns continued to be an issue as many lake and river levels dropped to low levels.  Reelfoot Lake was at one of the lowest levels in decades. Burning permits were required due to the drought and Tennessee State Parks banned backcountry campfires. A few wildfires occurred as a result of the dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions intensified to severe (D2).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on power lines in the Red Oak community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, October 26th, an intense surface low pressure center known as a midlatitude cyclone developed across the upper Midwest, resulting in gusty surface winds, which produced strong to high wind damage events at some locations across the Middle Tennessee. As the day progressed also, a strong cold frontal passage occurred. Associated with this frontal passage was an outbreak of severe weather, resulting in several counties experiencing thunderstorm wind damage along with funnel cloud reports, along with near golf ball size hail being reported in Coffee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few six inch tree limbs were downed at the Rushing Creek Campground near the Tennessee/Kentucky State Line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms brought isolated flooding reports to the mountains of east Tennessee during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department personnel reported 3 roads in Trade covered by high water from a slow moving thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued during the month of October across much of West Tennessee. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions from extreme portions of Northwest Tennessee to much of West Tennessee while conditions intensified across Northwest Tennessee to extreme (D3). The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed livestock with baled hay. Hydrological concerns continued to be an issue as many lake and river levels dropped to low levels.  Reelfoot Lake was at one of the lowest levels in decades. Burning permits were required due to the drought and Tennessee State Parks banned backcountry campfires. A few wildfires occurred as a result of the dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions became severe (D2) and eventually intensified further to extreme (D3).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary setup across Western and Middle Tennessee late Friday night (April 30), and remained through the weekend.  A persistent southerly flow fed moisture into the area and precipitable water values rose to almost 2 inches, based on data from KOHX upper air soundings.  As a series of shortwaves moved through, a band of showers and thunderstorms developed and remained nearly stationary for much of the day on Saturday, May 1st and Sunday, May 2nd, resulting in widespread record flooding across much of Middle Tennessee.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe also, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage and seven confirmed tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Peytonsville Road. Portions of Peytonsville Road had fallen into a nearby creek. Also, a fatality occurred when a man drove his vehicle into Garrison Creek at 12:30 PM CST.

The remnants of a tropical depression interacted with a stationary front in Middle Tennessee, resulting in excessive rainfall across several counties east of Interstate 65 corridor. Area averaged between six and seven inches of rain, with some areas receiving as much as a foot of rainfall. Most of the severe damage across this area included impacted bridges, roads, and multiple types of structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway 13 in the southern part of the county was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued during the month of October across much of West Tennessee. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions from extreme portions of Northwest Tennessee to much of West Tennessee while conditions intensified across Northwest Tennessee to extreme (D3). The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed livestock with baled hay. Hydrological concerns continued to be an issue as many lake and river levels dropped to low levels. Reelfoot Lake was at one of the lowest levels in decades. Burning permits were required due to the drought and Tennessee State Parks banned backcountry campfires. A few wildfires occurred as a result of the dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions intensified from severe (D2) to  extreme (D3).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers produced snowfall accumulation of 2 to 4 inches in portions of Franklin County.  Up to 4 inches of snow was reported in Sewanee at the University of the South.  The snow began on the morning of the 12th following the passage of a strong Arctic cold front, with periods of snow showers lasting off and on through the morning of the 13th.  Wind chills also dropped into the zero to 5 below zero range during the morning hours of the 13th due to gusty northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snowfall occurred in Franklin County.  Heaviest snowfall totals occurred across the northern and eastern portions of the county. These totals included:  4 inches at the Sewanee COOP and 3.1 inches at the Winchester COOP stations.  In the southern portions of the county, 1 to 3 inches of snowfall was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain produced flash flooding across Southern Shelby County. Numerous streets were flooded in the Germantown and Collierville areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary setup across Western and Middle Tennessee late Friday night (April 30), and remained through the weekend.  A persistent southerly flow fed moisture into the area and precipitable water values rose to almost 2 inches, based on data from KOHX upper air soundings.  As a series of shortwaves moved through, a band of showers and thunderstorms developed and remained nearly stationary for much of the day on Saturday, May 1st and Sunday, May 2nd, resulting in widespread record flooding across much of Middle Tennessee.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe also, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage and seven confirmed tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At U.S. Highway 431 near the Robertson/Davidson County Line at Sycamore Creek, water was overflowing its banks, resulting in flooding of low lying areas. At least one home and farm was reported to be covered with flood waters. At the time of this report, U.S. Highway 431 was still open, although the water level near the base of the road and bridge was rising fast. No additional information was provided concerning any damage to the road or bridge. Also, flooding was reported along Charlotte Road just west of downtown Nashville by the public. Emergency Manager reported closing of Bluff Road due to water covering the road. Water was reported over Culbertson Road along Mill Creek near Nolensville. This made the road impassible to traffic. On Estes Road, approximately one mile west of Green Hills, a creek was flowing over the road. Depth was estimated at three to six inches. This caused the road to become impassible to traffic. Water was running over Old Harding Pike from the Harpeth River near Morton Mill Road. Also, numerous homes, numerous businesses, numerous non comercial buildings, along with several county, state, and federal roads across the county received damage due to the flood. This damage also included major damage to the Opryland Hotel, Grand Ole Opry House, and Opry Mills shopping mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms developed across the western half of Middle Tennessee during the late morning through early afternoon hours on Tuesday, October 12th, some of these thunderstorms produced large hail in Hickman, Maury, and Williamson counties.

Below normal rainfall continued during the month of October across much of West Tennessee. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions from extreme portions of Northwest Tennessee to much of West Tennessee while conditions intensified across Northwest Tennessee to extreme (D3). The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed livestock with baled hay. Hydrological concerns continued to be an issue as many lake and river levels dropped to low levels.  Reelfoot Lake was at one of the lowest levels in decades. Burning permits were required due to the drought and Tennessee State Parks banned backcountry campfires. A few wildfires occurred as a result of the dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions intensified to severe (D2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain developed during the late morning and early afternoon hours as warm air aloft over-topped sub-freezing temperatures at ground-level.  Ice accumulations of up to a tenth of an inch were reported. Several local roads were closed on elevated surfaces and mountainous terrain.  Warmer temperatures spread into the area dramatically on the morning of the 16th with readings climbing into the 40s and 50s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing rain and sleet produced very hazardous travel conditions.   A few hundredths of an inch of ice were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon through evening hours on Saturday, October 24th, across locations mainly south of Interstate 40 and west of Interstate 24 in Middle Tennessee, at least a couple of well defined lines of showers and thunderstorms, with embedded severe thunderstorms within, moved across this region. This resulted in multiple reports of thunderstorm wind damage, two lightning strikes that caused considerable structural damage, and two observed funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike set fire to a house, whose original value was estimated to be near 2.45 million dollars, on Chatfield Way, resulting in a fire that initially broke through the roof and became visible from Cool Springs Boulevard before firefighters could get on the scene. This home received substantial fire damage. The home was located in Cool Springs in the Enclave at Cambridge Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers produced snowfall accumulation of an inch or less in Lincoln and Moore Counties.  The snow began on the morning of the 12th following the passage of a strong Arctic cold front, with periods of snow showers lasting off and on through the morning of the 13th.  Wind chills also dropped into the zero to 5 below zero range during the morning hours of the 13th due to gusty northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dusting to over one inch (1.2 inches) of snowfall was reported.

The storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds knocked down numerous trees and power lines across Northern Shelby County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst resulted in three barns with partial roof loss and an outbuilding along Unionville-Deason Road losing its entire roof. A couple dozen trees were snapped and many more had large limbs lost. The damage path was approximately 5 miles long and nearly half mile wide. Maximum wind speeds were estimated at around 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, October 26th, an intense surface low pressure center known as a midlatitude cyclone developed across the upper Midwest, resulting in gusty surface winds, which produced strong to high wind damage events at some locations across the Middle Tennessee. As the day progressed also, a strong cold frontal passage occurred. Associated with this frontal passage was an outbreak of severe weather, resulting in several counties experiencing thunderstorm wind damage along with funnel cloud reports, along with near golf ball size hail being reported in Coffee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a telephone pole line at the intersection of Austin and Browntown Roads near Sparta. Type and damage amount to telephone line was unkown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary setup across Western and Middle Tennessee late Friday night (April 30), and remained through the weekend.  A persistent southerly flow fed moisture into the area and precipitable water values rose to almost 2 inches, based on data from KOHX upper air soundings.  As a series of shortwaves moved through, a band of showers and thunderstorms developed and remained nearly stationary for much of the day on Saturday, May 1st and Sunday, May 2nd, resulting in widespread record flooding across much of Middle Tennessee.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe also, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage and seven confirmed tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short lived EF1 Tornado, with maximum winds of 100 MPH, touchdown in Hickman County three tenths of a mile north of the intersection of Ben Fly Road and Beech Valley Road. Path length was 2.73 miles, with a width of 50 yards. Numerous hard wood trees were uprooted and snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall washed boulders onto State Highway 52 just outside of Celina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heat wave occurred during the middle to latter part of June across the Mid-South as a strong upper ridge of high pressure moved over the region. Temperatures rose into the mid to upper 90s during the afternoon hours. The combination of highs in the mid to upper 90s and high humidity produced heat indices between 100 and 110 degrees. Overnight lows in the 70s to around 80 degrees provided little relief. Six heat-related deaths occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged from 100 to 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was underneath northwest flow aloft during the period between July 11th, 2010 and July 13th, 2010. Several upper level disturbances quickly moved through the flow setting off complexes of showers and thunderstorms. Some of these showers and thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding from Downtown Memphis to East Memphis and Cordova. Interstate 240 at Union Avenue was flooded as well as North Parkway at North Dunlap and at Cleveland. The intersection of Sycamore View and Summer Avenue was flooded. One vehicle was stuck in the flooded intersection of Tchulahoma Road and Knight Arnold Road while two cars were stranded in flood water on Hardin Avenue. In addition, the intersection of McLean Boulevard and Evelyn Avenue was flooded as well as Walnut Grove Road near Germantown Parkway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported downed in Smyrna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms intially developed during the early afternoon, but became more widespread across central portions of Middle Tennessee during the early evening hours on June 26th. These severe thunderstorms produced thunderstorm wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey team consisting of personnel from the NWS and the Metro Nashville Office of Emergency Management surveyed damage in the Grieve Hall area of Southern Davidson County. A microburst, with maximum winds around 90 mph occurred. It resulted in around 100 trees being snapped or uprooted, several buildings with partial uplifting and shingle damage, and four wooden power poles being snapped. Initial damage was noted at the corner of Harding Place and Trousdale Drive. A couple of windows were blown out of a local businesses, several signs were destroyed, and a gas pump was blown over onto a car. The damage area then extended around one mile to the east and south, generally an area bordered by Elysian Fields Road to the north and Trousdale Road to the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Coffee and Cumberland Counties during the late afternoon hours on June 15th, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage, large hail, and a lightning strike causing major damage to a barn respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed along Blanton Chapel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Coffee and Cumberland Counties during the late afternoon hours on June 15th, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage, large hail, and a lightning strike causing major damage to a barn respectively.

Severe thunderstorms developed across Coffee and Cumberland Counties during the late afternoon hours on June 15th, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage, large hail, and a lightning strike causing major damage to a barn respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a barn and set fire to it, causing major damage. The barn was located  on U.S. Highway 127 South, just south of the Cumberland Mountain State Park. A house was also struck by lightning on U.S. Highway 127 South, but no fire was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge of high pressure built over the Mid-South from July 24th through July 26th, 2010. Meanwhile, a surface boundary sagged further south into West Tennessee that allowed higher dewpoints to pool along the boundary. The combination of the heat from the ridge and the higher dewpoints produced heat index readings between 105-109 degrees during this period. One death occurred as a result of the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged between 105-109 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved southeast into the Mid-South and interacted with remnants of Tropical Depression Bonnie as the remnants moved north. Numerous showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and evening hours of July 25th-July 26th, 2010 as a result. Some storms became severe and produced damaging winds, flash flooding. Behind the front during the evening of July 26th, a wake low developed that produced high winds over portions of Northwest Tennessee resulting in additional wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees along Highway 114 and Stage Road in Scotts Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered supercell thunderstorms produced large hail and a tornado during the late afternoon hours ahead of a cold front.  The tornadic storm affected southeastern Franklin County including Sewanee.  The storm environment exhibited strong wind shear and moderate instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on the west side of Jump Off road near the Sewanee Airport.  Numerous large trees were snapped or uprooted. The tornado continued to move northeast producing minor damage to homes and snapping trees along Leaky Pond Lane. Along Otter Falls Lane in the Midway community, a home sustained significant structural damage when the carport and chimney were destroyed by flying debris.  ||The tornado continued to produce minor damage to a few homes and sporadic tree damage as it continued moving northeast toward the Marion county line.  One resident reported hearing and seeing very large hail just before the tornado.  Their home sustained roof damage and the driveway was damaged by a falling tree. The tornado eventually weakened as it tracked into Marion county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heat wave occurred during the middle to latter part of June across the Mid-South as a strong upper ridge of high pressure moved over the region. Temperatures rose into the mid to upper 90s during the afternoon hours. The combination of highs in the mid to upper 90s and high humidity produced heat indices between 100 and 110 degrees. Overnight lows in the 70s to around 80 degrees provided little relief. Six heat-related deaths occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged from 100 to 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours, thunderstorms developed that produced hail ranging from penny to golf ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered large hail and damaging wind occurred along and ahead of a weak cold front that was moving into east and southeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust was reported near the intersection of Highway 33 and Satterfield road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two isolated severe thunderstorms formed in an unstable atmosphere during the evening resulting in wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed by thunderstorm winds on Gamble Gap road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Vorticity lobe rotated northeast across East Tennessee in larger parent circulation around Northwest Missouri upper level low pressure system. Convection became severe producing some large hail and wind damage in an area where cold air aloft was coincident with a low level jet and zone of low level convergence ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down in Knoxville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated convection developed in the Tennessee Valley producing damaging outflow gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in Fountain City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability with rather steep lapse rates and sufficient shear developed ahead of a cold front that supported the development of hail producing convection mainly on the higher Cumberland Plateau terrain and nearby counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail fell four miles east of Decatur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low located over eastern sections of the Mid South combined with an outflow boundary draped across the area resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms over parts of West Tennessee. A few of these storms were severe with large hail during the evening hours.

A cold front tracked through the region triggering scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Storm reports contained mainly damaging thunderstorm wind, but an EF2 tornado formed in Hamilton County.  Six people were injured.  Tornadoes also formed in Bradley, Loudon and Marion Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department personnel reported a few trees downed by thunderstorm winds in Norris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Saturday, September 11th, a line of showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of this cold front. Some of the thunderstorms within this line reached severe thresholds, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage in four counties across the mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and limbs were blown down from the Warner Park area to Oak Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak short wave trough moved east across the mid south generating convection ahead of an associated cold frontal boundary. Instability was weak but moderate westerly flow allowed for sufficient shear. Scattered severe convection developed during the late afternoon and early evening across Eastern Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were reported down in South Knoxville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms brought isolated flooding reports to the mountains of east Tennessee during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced flooding, including damaging the floor at the local YMCA building in Erwin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, October 26th, an intense surface low pressure center known as a midlatitude cyclone developed across the upper Midwest, resulting in gusty surface winds, which produced strong to high wind damage events at some locations across the Middle Tennessee. As the day progressed also, a strong cold frontal passage occurred. Associated with this frontal passage was an outbreak of severe weather, resulting in several counties experiencing thunderstorm wind damage along with funnel cloud reports, along with near golf ball size hail being reported in Coffee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a funnel cloud over Collinwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary setup across Western and Middle Tennessee late Friday night (April 30), and remained through the weekend.  A persistent southerly flow fed moisture into the area and precipitable water values rose to almost 2 inches, based on data from KOHX upper air soundings.  As a series of shortwaves moved through, a band of showers and thunderstorms developed and remained nearly stationary for much of the day on Saturday, May 1st and Sunday, May 2nd, resulting in widespread record flooding across much of Middle Tennessee.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe also, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage and seven confirmed tornadoes across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on U.S. Highway 412 just west of Linden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved southwest to northeast across Middle Tennessee on May 14th, producing flash flooding, hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and a lightning strike to a structure that caused damage in Macon County.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved southwest to northeast across Middle Tennessee on May 14th, producing flash flooding, hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and a lightning strike to a structure that caused damage in Macon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff's Office reported that Interstate 24 at Exit 57 was flooded with 8 inches of water on the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado with max winds of 70 mph moved through this rural area, twisting and downing a few trees in its short lived path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary lingering across the area triggered diurnal thunderstorms during the heat of day. Some storms became severe producing damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotters reported thunderstorms produced quarter-size hail in Farragut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across three counties in the eastern half of Middle Tennessee through the afternoon into the early evening hours on June 24th. These severe thunderstorms produced thunderstorm wind damage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in the East Northfield/Huntington Drive area.  A traffic light was also damaged.  Extent of damage to traffic light was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary lingering across the area triggered diurnal thunderstorms during the heat of day. Some storms became severe producing damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported numerous trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds across much of the western half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms formed in a moisture rich airmass across Middle Tennessee ahead of an approaching frontal system during the evening hours on Monday, July 12th. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, producing thunderstorm wind, flash flood, heavy rain, and lightning damage across several counties in the mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees were downed along U.S. Highway 70 in Elmwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on June 9th.  Some of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, resulting mainly in thunderstorm wind damage across several counties across the mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down across the entire county.  Most recent report received at this time was a tree blocking State Highway 96 just east of the Arrington Post Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level disturbance combined with a very unstable air mass to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Mid South on May 24th, 2010.  As convection developed throughout the day outflow boundaries were created that resulted in additional development until nearly all of the Mid South experienced at least some precipitation by sunset. After sunset the storms dissipated. Some of the storms were severe with large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
Low Pressure moved west of Middle Tennessee during the late afternoon and evening of April 24th.  A line of severe thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee ahead of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wilson county emergency management agency reported that lightning started a house and field fire on Terrance Hill road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary triggered isolated severe thunderstorm across southeast Tennessee during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company personnel reported a few trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds in Collegedale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located over the Mid-South June 2nd-June 3rd, 2010. The front was the focus of afternoon and evening thunderstorm development. Some of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Friendship. Walter Taylor Road was flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked through the region triggering scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Storm reports contained mainly damaging thunderstorm wind, but an EF2 tornado formed in Hamilton County.  Six people were injured.  Tornadoes also formed in Bradley, Loudon and Marion Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department personnel reported a few trees downed by thunderstorm winds in La Follette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, October 26th, an intense surface low pressure center known as a midlatitude cyclone developed across the upper Midwest, resulting in gusty surface winds, which produced strong to high wind damage events at some locations across the Middle Tennessee. As the day progressed also, a strong cold frontal passage occurred. Associated with this frontal passage was an outbreak of severe weather, resulting in several counties experiencing thunderstorm wind damage along with funnel cloud reports, along with near golf ball size hail being reported in Coffee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail almost golf ball size 2 miles east of the Interstate 24 Beechgrove Exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Depression 5 moved northward into the Mid-South during the day on August 18th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the Tropical Depression 5. Some storms produced very heavy rain which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced widespread flash flooding in Hardin County. Several roads in Savannah were closed as a result including Highway 64 which was covered by at least six inches of water. At least 5 homes along Hwy 64 in Olive Hill sustained flood damage. Numerous roads and bridges sustained damage from the flooding. One man was rescued from his vehicle after being trapped from flood waters on Lonesome Pine Road. One other motorist had to be rescued as well. Roads were also closed northeast of Savannah where water was as high as two feet on some roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave tracking through the area in an unstable environment triggered scattered thunderstorms during the early morning hours. Most of the storm reports contained damaging thunderstorm winds, but an EF 0 tornado was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio personnel reported trees and traffic lights downed by thunderstorm winds in Cleveland and across numerous areas of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms developed across the western half of Middle Tennessee during the late morning through early afternoon hours on Tuesday, October 12th, some of these thunderstorms produced large hail in Hickman, Maury, and Williamson counties.
A boundary across the area triggered isolated severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported several trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds in Kingsport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe convection developed along a stationary frontal boundary in place across East Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours. Development was aided by moderate to strong instability in the presence of a weak short wave trough migrating east across Northern Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down near Rockwood.
